article_title: NASA Taps Relativity Space to Privately Build and Fly a 2028 Mars Orbiter Carrying the Aeolus Atmospheric Suite
summary: Submission approvable with 1 minor warning(s) — file a corrections record on merge

content_preview.title: NASA Taps Relativity Space to Privately Build and Fly a 2028 Mars Orbiter Carrying the Aeolus Atmospheric Suite
content_preview.summary: NASA will supply the four-instrument Aeolus suite while Relativity Space builds and launches the orbiter under the agency's first six-year reimbursable Space Act Agreement.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

NASA on June 17, 2026 announced a public-private partnership with Relativity Space to advance Mars science, with the agency supplying a four-instrument atmospheric payload called Aeolus while the company builds the spacecraft and launches it toward the Red Planet, according to [NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-announces-public-private-partnership-to-advance-mars-science/). The mission is scheduled to launch in 2028 and is structured under what NASA describes as its first...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Clear, well-structured News piece (492 words, within the 400-1200 News range). Overview / What We Know / What We Don't Know structure is appropriate, and the article is careful to attribute each claim to a named source inline.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 4 source snapshots read from disk and verified. source-0 (NASA news release, 200): confirms verbatim the four instruments and their parenthetical codes — Doppler Wind and Temperature Sounder (DWTS-Ozone), Thermal Limb Sounder (TLS), Surface Radiometric Sensor Package (SuRSeP), Wide-Field Context Camera (WFCC) — plus the 2028 launch, NASA's 'first six-year reimbursable Space Act Agreement' framing, the Ames Research Center / Silicon Valley payload role, the Relativity spacecraft+rocket+ops role, the 'at least one Martian year' support commitment, Administrator Jared Isaacman's name, and the full 'force multiplier for science' quote word-for-word. source-1 (Scientific American, 200): confirms the two-stage reusable Terran R, its debut targeted for late 2026, 'have not yet reached orbit,' Eric Schmidt as CEO since March 2025, the 2028 launch target, the 'reduce risk to landings on Mars - both crewed and uncrewed' goal, and 'temperature, dust, wind and cloud conditions.' source-2 (Universe Magazine, 200): confirms Relativity's only launch was March 2023 with Terran 1 passing max aerodynamic pressure before the second-stage engine shut down and the vehicle did not reach orbit, and NASA funding/maintaining instruments for at least one Martian year. source-3 (ExecutiveGov, 200): confirms NASA committed to support Aeolus instruments operations for one Martian year.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Every specific (four instrument codes, 2028 timeframe, six-year reimbursable Space Act Agreement framing, Terran R not-yet-flown status, late-2026 debut, March 2023 Terran 1 outcome, Isaacman quote) traces to a cited snapshot. No fabrication detected. The article correctly states in 'What We Don't Know' that no private funding backer is named — verified: none of the sources name a philanthropic/private backer, so the article does not invent one.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and factual throughout. No sensationalism, no AI self-reference. Caveats (unflown rocket, no named backer) are handled responsibly in a dedicated section.
editor_notes.originality: Distinct from the June 9 MAVEN decommissioning article (2026-06/09-nasa-declares-maven-mars-orbiter-dead-...), which covers the end of an existing orbiter; this covers a new 2028 mission. The inline cross-reference link resolves to an existing published article. Not a duplicate.
concerns: Two of the four sources (universemagazine.com, executivegov.com) are not on config/source_allowlist.txt. They only corroborate facts already established by the allowlisted NASA news release and Scientific American — the headline, summary, lead, instrument names, and Isaacman quote all rest on allowlisted sources — so the allowlist gap does not affect any load-bearing claim.
recommendations: File a corrections note recording that two corroborating sources fall outside the editorial allowlist, per the automated recommendation.
Maintainers may consider adding universemagazine.com and executivegov.com to the allowlist if they are deemed reputable for future space coverage.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Substantively accurate and well-sourced submission. The only issue is the allowlist warning on two corroborating sources, which is recoverable via a public corrections note. Verdict: APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS.
